data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-07T15:54:01.057Z</current_datetime>

I have a test suite that sometimes passes and sometimes fails depending on test execution order.
Can you look at the test code and figure out what's going on?

```csharp
// OrderServiceTests.cs
using Microsoft.VisualStudio.TestTools.UnitTesting;
using System.Reflection;

namespace FlakyCoupled.Tests;

[TestClass]
public class OrderServiceTests
{
    private static List<string> _processedOrders = new();

    [TestInitialize]
    public void Setup()
    {
        // Note: _processedOrders is never cleared between tests
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void Test1()
    {
        var service = new OrderService();
        var order = service.CreateOrder("item-1", 2);
        _processedOrders.Add(order.Id);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order);
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void Test2()
    {
        Assert.AreEqual(1, _processedOrders.Count);
        var service = new OrderService();
        service.ProcessOrder(_processedOrders[0]);
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void ProcessOrder_SetsTimestamp()
    {
        var service = new OrderService();
        var order = service.CreateOrder("item-2", 1);
        service.ProcessOrder(order.Id);

        Thread.Sleep(2000);

        var processed = service.GetOrder(order.Id);
        Assert.IsTrue(processed.ProcessedAt.Value.Day == DateTime.Now.Day);
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void CreateOrder_InternalState_IsCorrect()
    {
        var service = new OrderService();
        var order = service.CreateOrder("item-3", 5);

        var field = typeof(OrderService).GetField("_orders", BindingFlags.NonPublic | BindingFlags.Instance);
        var orders = (Dictionary<string, Order>)field.GetValue(service);
        Assert.AreEqual(1, orders.Count);
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void CreateOrder_MultipleItems_Test()
    {
        var service = new OrderService();
        var order1 = service.CreateOrder("item-a", 1);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order1);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order1.Id);
        var order2 = service.CreateOrder("item-b", 2);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order2);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order2.Id);
        var order3 = service.CreateOrder("item-c", 3);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order3);
        Assert.IsNotNull(order3.Id);
        Assert.AreNotEqual(order1.Id, order2.Id);
        Assert.AreNotEqual(order2.Id, order3.Id);
        Assert.AreNotEqual(order1.Id, order3.Id);
        var all = service.GetAllOrders();
        Assert.AreEqual(3, all.Count);
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Any(o => o.ItemName == "item-a"));
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Any(o => o.ItemName == "item-b"));
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Any(o => o.ItemName == "item-c"));
        Assert.IsTrue(all.All(o => o.Quantity > 0));
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Sum(o => o.Quantity) == 6);
        Console.WriteLine($"Created {all.Count} orders successfully");
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void ProcessOrder_InvalidId_Throws()
    {
        var service = new OrderService();
        Assert.ThrowsException<Exception>(() => service.ProcessOrder("nonexistent"));
    }
}
```

data.content:
The root cause is **shared mutable static state** creating an inter-test dependency:

```csharp
private static List<string> _processedOrders = new();
```

**`Test2` depends on `Test1` running first.** It asserts `_processedOrders.Count == 1` and reads `_processedOrders[0]`, both of which only work if `Test1` already ran and added an item. If the runner executes `Test2` first → index out of range / count mismatch.

The `Setup()` method even has a comment acknowledging the list is never cleared, yet does nothing about it.

### Other issues worth noting

| Test | Problem |
|---|---|
| `ProcessOrder_SetsTimestamp` | Compares `.Day` after a `Thread.Sleep(2000)` — fails if run near midnight when the day rolls over. |
| `CreateOrder_InternalState_IsCorrect` | Uses reflection on a private field (`_orders`) — brittle to internal refactors, though not order-dependent. |

### Fix

Make each test self-contained. `Test2` should create its own order instead of relying on `Test1`'s side effect, and the static list should be removed (or at minimum cleared in `Setup`). The timestamp test should compare against a captured "before" time rather than relying on calendar-day equality.
data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to analyze flaky test code. Let me look at it carefully without needing to search the filesystem since the code is provided inline.

The issues are clear from reading the code:

1. **Shared mutable static state (`_processedOrders`)**: This `static List<string>` is shared across all tests but never cleared. `Test2` depends on `Test1` having run first (it asserts `_processedOrders.Count == 1`). If `Test2` runs before `Test1`, the list is empty and it fails. If other tests run before `Test2` that also add to the list, the count won't be 1.


2. **`Test2` explicitly depends on `Test1`'s side effects**: It reads from `_processedOrders[0]` which was added by `Test1`.
3. **`ProcessOrder_SetsTimestamp`**: Uses `Thread.Sleep(2000)` and then checks `processed.ProcessedAt.Value.Day == DateTime.Now.Day`. This could fail around midnight when the day changes between processing and assertion.
4. **`CreateOrder_InternalState_IsCorrect`**: Uses reflection to access private field `_orders` - this is brittle and will break if the implementation changes, though it's not order-dependent.

The core issue is that `Test1` and `Test2` are tightly coupled through shared static state, which is the primary source of flakiness.
